lines.original: Show Mxrci on it! he-he-he!
lines.literal: Show Mxrci on it! he-he-he!
lines.natural: Put 'Mxrci' on it! he-he-he!
lines.slangAware: Put 'Mxrci' on it! he-he-he!
lines.chosen: Put 'Mxrci' on it! he-he-he!
lines.translated: Put 'Mxrci' on it! he-he-he!
lines.note: 'Mxrci' appears to be a stylized spelling (likely 'Merci' / Mercedes reference).

lines.original: (Haan)
lines.literal: (Haan)
lines.natural: (Yes)
lines.slangAware: (Yes)
lines.chosen: Yes
lines.translated: Yes

lines.original: Do do ghode Magna ni
lines.literal: Do do ghode Magna ni
lines.natural: I don't ask for two horses.
lines.slangAware: I don't ask for two horses.
lines.chosen: I don't ask for two horses.
lines.translated: I don't ask for two horses.

lines.original: Char G-Wagon'an ni
lines.literal: Char G-Wagon'an ni
lines.natural: Four G-Wagons.
lines.slangAware: Four G-Wagons.
lines.chosen: Four G-Wagons.
lines.translated: Four G-Wagons.
lines.note: 'G-Wagon' refers to the Mercedes G-Class SUV; 'ni' here is likely a colloquial particle.
lines.ambiguity: The particle 'ni' can be emphatic or negative in different contexts; here it reads as emphatic/listing.

lines.original: Utte nu main chhe chhe inch chakkiyan
lines.literal: Utte nu main chhe chhe inch chakkiyan
lines.natural: I put six-inch chakkiyan on them.
lines.slangAware: I put six-inch chakkiyan on them.
lines.chosen: On which I place six-inch wheels
lines.translated: On which I place six-inch wheels
lines.note: 'chakkiyan' is unclear in this context — could refer to rims, pipes, or another car part; 'chhe chhe inch' = 'six six-inch' (i.e., six-inch).
lines.ambiguity: Unclear what 'chakkiyan' specifically denotes (rims, exhausts, grinders, etc.).

lines.original: Time laggu mitne nu
lines.literal: Time laggu mitne nu
lines.natural: It'll take time to get rid of them.
lines.slangAware: It'll take time to get rid of them.
lines.chosen: It'll take time to get rid of them.
lines.translated: It'll take time to get rid of them.
lines.note: 'mitne' likely from 'mitana' (to erase/remove/settle).
lines.ambiguity: Could mean 'erase', 'defeat', or 'settle' depending on intent.

lines.original: Sanu thalle sittne nu
lines.literal: Sanu thalle sittne nu
lines.natural: To bring us down.
lines.slangAware: To bring us down.
lines.chosen: To bring us down.
lines.translated: To bring us down.
lines.ambiguity: The verb 'sittne' could mean 'to make sit' or more generally 'to put down/force down'; reading here is 'bring us down'.

lines.original: Ikki vaari jor laa lai chhatti'an
lines.literal: Ikki vaari jor laa lai chhatti'an
lines.natural: Give one strong push to their chests.
lines.slangAware: Give one strong push to their chests.
lines.chosen: Give one strong push to their chests.
lines.translated: Give one strong push to their chests.
lines.note: Words are uncertain: 'Ikki' can mean 'once' or 'twenty-one'; 'chhatti'an' could be 'chests' (chhatiyaan) or 'roofs' (chhattian) depending on pronunciation.
lines.ambiguity: Multiple readings: number ('ikki' = once or 21) and target ('chhatti'an' = chests vs. roofs). Exact intended meaning is unclear.

lines.original: Fan ikko naar da main
lines.literal: I am a fan of only one woman.
lines.natural: I'm only a fan of one woman.
lines.slangAware: I'm only a fan of one woman.
lines.chosen: I'm only a fan of one woman.
lines.translated: I'm only a fan of one woman.
lines.note: Here 'fan' is likely the English loanword meaning 'admirer/supporter'.
lines.ambiguity: ‘Fan’ could be interpreted as the English loanword 'fan' (admirer) or less likely a different Punjabi word; chosen assumes the English loanword.

lines.original: Das vaari vaarda main
lines.literal: I say it ten times.
lines.natural: I say it ten times.
lines.slangAware: I say it ten times.
lines.chosen: I say it ten times.
lines.translated: I say it ten times.
lines.ambiguity: The verb 'vaarda' could mean 'say', 'shout', or another repeated action (e.g., 'do' or 'hit') — translated conservatively as 'say'.

lines.original: Ohde utton sau-sau diyan gatthiyan
lines.literal: Hundreds and hundreds of knots on/over her.
lines.natural: Hundreds of knots over her.
lines.slangAware: Hundreds of knots over her.
lines.chosen: Hundreds of knots over her.
lines.translated: Hundreds of knots over her.

lines.original: Time laggu mitne nu
lines.literal: It will take time to remove (them).
lines.natural: It will take time to remove them.
lines.slangAware: It will take time to remove them.
lines.chosen: It will take time to remove them.
lines.translated: It will take time to remove them.

lines.original: Sanu thalle sittne nu
lines.literal: For us to be put down / brought low.
lines.natural: to bring us down.
lines.slangAware: to bring us down.
lines.chosen: to bring us down.
lines.translated: to bring us down.
lines.ambiguity: The word 'sittne' is uncertain in spelling/meaning here; it may mean 'to be set down', 'to be made sit', or idiomatically 'to be brought down' or 'humiliated'. Chosen a conservative reading: 'bring us down.'

lines.original: Ikki vaari jor laa lai chhatti
lines.literal: Put/press force once on the chest.
lines.natural: Give one strong push to the chest.
lines.slangAware: Give one strong push to the chest.
lines.chosen: Give one strong push to the chest.
lines.translated: Give one strong push to the chest.
lines.note: Phrase is idiomatic; 'jor laa' = apply force; 'chhatti' likely refers to chest/upper body but could be dialectal.
lines.ambiguity: Unclear if 'chhatti' here means 'chest' or another regional term (or a numeric reference); translation uses the common meaning 'chest'.

lines.original: Vall kaddta rekaane hunn tutte na crease
lines.literal: Pulled out the edge/hem, now the seams won't tear/crease.
lines.natural: He pulled out the hem, so the seams won't tear or crease now.
lines.slangAware: He pulled out the hem, so the seams won't tear or crease now.
lines.chosen: He pulled out the hem, so the seams won't tear or crease now.
lines.translated: He pulled out the hem, so the seams won't tear or crease now.
lines.note: 'rekaane' likely refers to 'rekaan' (seams/stitches) — clothing imagery.
lines.ambiguity: Some verbs and compounds ('vall kaddta rekaane') are regional/slang; rendered conservatively as physical action on clothing (pulling out hem/seam).

lines.original: Thoda rukhe aa subaah de sanu aaundi ni tameez
lines.literal: You're a bit rough in the mornings; we don't have manners.
lines.natural: You're a little rough in the mornings; we don't have manners.
lines.slangAware: You're a little rough in the mornings; we don't have manners.
lines.chosen: You're a little rough in the mornings; we don't have manners.
lines.translated: You're a little rough in the mornings; we don't have manners.
lines.note: 'tameez' means 'manners' or 'politeness'.
lines.ambiguity: Could mean 'you're a little coarse in the morning' or 'mornings are a bit rough for you'; chosen reads as addressing someone being rough in mornings and stating 'we don't have manners.'

lines.original: Aa lai tainu ditti saambh laa ni bebe wali chunni
lines.literal: Here, take it — I gave you, keep it, the girl-ish scarf.
lines.natural: Here, take it; I gave it to you — keep that girl's scarf safe.
lines.slangAware: Here, take it; I gave it to you — keep that girl's scarf safe.
lines.chosen: Here, take it; I gave it to you — keep that girl's scarf safe.
lines.translated: Here, take it; I gave it to you — keep that girl's scarf safe.
lines.note: bebe ~ beeba/biba = girl / dear girl (see glossary).

lines.original: Baitha double R vich paake bapu di kameez
lines.literal: Sitting in Double R wearing father's shirt.
lines.natural: Sitting in a Double R wearing my father's shirt.
lines.slangAware: Sitting in a Double R wearing my father's shirt.
lines.chosen: Sitting in a Double R wearing my father's shirt.
lines.translated: Sitting in a Double R wearing my father's shirt.
lines.note: “Double R” likely refers to a vehicle brand abbreviated RR (e.g., Rolls‑Royce or similar); exact referent is ambiguous.
lines.ambiguity: ‘Double R’ could mean Rolls‑Royce, Range Rover, or another RR‑branded vehicle — text doesn't specify which.

lines.original: Bahla door ni rekaane hun Ranjheyan nu kheda
lines.literal: Maybe don't keep them far now — play with the Ranjhas.
lines.natural: Maybe don't keep them at a distance now; mess/play with those Ranjhas.
lines.slangAware: Maybe don't keep them at a distance now; mess/play with those Ranjhas.
lines.chosen: Maybe don't keep them at a distance now; mess with those Ranjhas.
lines.translated: Maybe don't keep them at a distance now; mess with those Ranjhas.
lines.note: Ranjheyan (Ranjhas) can refer to lovers or romantic rivals (from Punjabi folklore).
lines.ambiguity: The verb 'kheda' can mean 'play with', 'toy with', or 'mess with' someone; tone/context isn't explicit.

lines.original: Bahla nede nu na jawan bas jhaaka ee bada mera
lines.literal: Maybe I won't go near — just one big look of mine.
lines.natural: Maybe I won't come close; one look from me is enough.
lines.slangAware: Maybe I won't come close; one look from me is enough.
lines.chosen: Maybe I won't come close; one look from me is enough.
lines.translated: Maybe I won't come close; one look from me is enough.

lines.original: Bahla ruka na rekaane kite maaran ikk fera
lines.literal: Maybe don't stop/hold them, otherwise they might hit once on return.
lines.natural: Maybe don't try to hold them back — they might come back and strike once.
lines.slangAware: Maybe don't try to hold them back — they might come back and strike once.
lines.chosen: Maybe don't try to hold them back — they might come back and strike once.
lines.translated: Maybe don't try to hold them back — they might come back and strike once.
lines.ambiguity: Phrases around 'ruka na rekaane' and 'maar an ikk fera' are colloquial; could mean 'don't stop them' or 'if not stopped they might retaliate once.'

lines.original: Naddi do vall kha gayi ditta ikk vaari geda
lines.literal: The stream/river took (it) to both sides and gave one spin/turn.
lines.natural: The stream swept it to both sides and made it spin once.
lines.slangAware: The stream swept it to both sides and made it spin once.
lines.chosen: The stream swept it to both sides and made it spin once.
lines.translated: The stream swept it to both sides and made it spin once.
lines.note: Naddi = stream/river; 'do vall' = both sides; 'geda' = spin/turn. Exact referent (vehicle, person, object) is unclear from this line alone.
lines.ambiguity: Unclear what was taken/spun by the stream (vehicle, person, object); wording is idiomatic/colloquial.

lines.original: Kithe dabeya rekaane ehna dekh layi dabake
lines.literal: Where did I press/hold to keep them? See, I pressed them down.
lines.natural: Where did I press to hold them? See—I pressed them down.
lines.slangAware: Where did I press to hold them? See—I pressed them down.
lines.chosen: Where did I press to hold them? See—I pressed them down.
lines.translated: Where did I press to hold them? See—I pressed them down.
lines.ambiguity: Phrase is colloquial; could be literal pressing or figurative 'keeping someone under control' — context unclear.

lines.original: Karaan shopping'an Milan Afghan 'ali kha ke
lines.literal: I do shopping — Milan, Afghani, 'Ali — after eating.
lines.natural: I go shopping: Milan, Afghani, 'Ali — after I've eaten.
lines.slangAware: I go shopping: Milan, Afghani, 'Ali — after I've eaten.
lines.chosen: I go shopping: Milan, Afghani, 'Ali — after I've eaten.
lines.translated: I go shopping: Milan, Afghani, 'Ali — after I've eaten.
lines.note: This line lists names/places (Milan, Afghani, 'Ali) that may refer to brands, locations, or foods; exact sense is unclear.
lines.ambiguity: Unclear whether Milan/Afghan/'Ali are stores, locations, cuisines, or brands; word order and punctuation in original make parse uncertain.

lines.original: Tera darji rekaane change karde Mohali
lines.literal: Your tailor does good alterations in Mohali
lines.natural: Your tailor does good alterations in Mohali.
lines.slangAware: Your tailor does good alterations in Mohali.
lines.chosen: Your tailor does good alterations in Mohali.
lines.translated: Your tailor does good alterations in Mohali.

lines.original: Jutti pairan wich paavan tere Paris'on manga ke
lines.literal: I put shoes on my feet that I ordered from Paris for you
lines.natural: I wear shoes I ordered from Paris for you.
lines.slangAware: I wear shoes I ordered from Paris for you.
lines.chosen: I wear shoes I ordered from Paris for you.
lines.translated: I wear shoes I ordered from Paris for you.
lines.ambiguity: It’s unclear whether "tere" (for you) means the shoes were ordered on your behalf or that he’s addressing someone while saying he wears Paris-ordered shoes.

lines.original: Tin mahine tin change ne location'an rekaane
lines.literal: Three months three changes are the locations I arrange
lines.natural: I change locations every three months.
lines.slangAware: I change locations every three months.
lines.chosen: I change locations every three months.
lines.translated: I change locations every three months.
lines.ambiguity: Phrase could mean "three months, three location changes" (i.e., three moves within three months) or simply "I change locations every three months." Chosen is the conservative read.

lines.original: Nitt UAE ton UK nu rotation'an rekaane
lines.literal: Daily I arrange rotations from the UAE to the UK
lines.natural: I regularly rotate between the UAE and the UK.
lines.slangAware: I regularly rotate between the UAE and the UK.
lines.chosen: I regularly rotate between the UAE and the UK.
lines.translated: I regularly rotate between the UAE and the UK.

lines.original: Main taan rukja dimag sala rukda ni mera
lines.literal: I really don't stop; my damn mind doesn't stop
lines.natural: I don't stop; my mind never stops.
lines.slangAware: I don't stop; my mind never stops.
lines.chosen: I don't stop; my mind never stops.
lines.translated: I don't stop; my mind never stops.
lines.note: “sala” is a colloquial intensifier here (mild expletive used for emphasis).

lines.original: Tan hi laa laiye samadhi meditation'an rekaane
lines.literal: So we even bring samadhi meditation sessions
lines.natural: So we even bring samadhi meditation sessions.
lines.slangAware: So we even bring samadhi meditation sessions.
lines.chosen: So we even bring samadhi meditation sessions.
lines.translated: So we even bring samadhi meditation sessions.
lines.note: “samadhi” refers to a deep meditative state/term from Indic spiritual practice.

lines.original: Loki sade kolon sikh hunn sanu den mattan
lines.literal: People learn from us now, they don't give us (anything)
lines.natural: People learn from us, but they don't give us anything.
lines.slangAware: People learn from us, but they don't give us anything.
lines.chosen: People learn from us, but they don't give us anything.
lines.translated: People learn from us, but they don't give us anything.
lines.ambiguity: The phrase could imply "they learn from us and don't return the favor" or simply "they take from us and give nothing back."

lines.original: Sanu kinna kuj de giyan ni mitti diyan vattan
lines.literal: They haven't given us much — (only) earthen pots/clods of dirt
lines.natural: They gave us almost nothing — only earthen pots / clods of dirt.
lines.slangAware: They gave us almost nothing — only earthen pots / clods of dirt.
lines.chosen: They gave us almost nothing — only earthen pots (clods of dirt).
lines.translated: They gave us almost nothing — only earthen pots (clods of dirt).
lines.note: Translation of "mitti diyan vattan" is uncertain; literally it's about earth/clay items ("earthen pots" or "clods of dirt").
lines.ambiguity: Unclear whether this means literally they gave clay pots, or figuratively they gave worthless things; exact phrasing is idiomatic.

lines.original: Jithon langhe tera yaar maare camera flash
lines.literal: Where your friend passes, he hits camera flash.
lines.natural: Your friend flashes the camera wherever he goes.
lines.slangAware: Your friend flashes the camera wherever he goes.
lines.chosen: Your friend flashes the camera wherever he goes.
lines.translated: Your friend flashes the camera wherever he goes.

lines.original: Hun khinch'de ne photo kade khinch de si lattan
lines.literal: Now photos are being taken; sometimes they used to take lattan.
lines.natural: Now they're taking photos; sometimes they used to take 'lattan'.
lines.slangAware: Now they're taking photos; sometimes they used to take 'lattan'.
lines.chosen: Now they're taking photos; sometimes they used to take 'lattan'.
lines.translated: Now they're taking photos; sometimes they used to take 'lattan'.
lines.note: The word 'lattan' is unclear in this line—may be slang or a local term not obvious from context.
lines.ambiguity: The phrase 'kade khinch de si lattan' is ambiguous: it could refer to a specific kind of photo, a local slang action, or be a mis-romanization. I kept a conservative literal rendering rather than guessing a poetic sense.

lines.original: Kehndi akh na lagge ve jad di mari tere utte
lines.literal: She says 'may the evil eye not fall' when (it) was struck on you.
lines.natural: She says, "May the evil eye not affect you," when something struck you.
lines.slangAware: She says, "May the evil eye not affect you," when something struck you.
lines.chosen: She says, "May the evil eye not affect you," when something struck you.
lines.translated: She says, "May the evil eye not affect you," when something struck you.
lines.ambiguity: The clause 'jad di mari tere utte' is unclear: it could mean 'when someone was struck (with love/attention) by you' or 'when something (like a look or charm) affected you.' I chose a conservative, literal reading.

lines.original: Sanu sidha ne banage ni halaat sade puthe
lines.literal: We have not become straight; (that's) the state of our sons.
lines.natural: We haven't become straight/upstanding; that's the state of our boys.
lines.slangAware: We haven't become straight/upstanding; that's the state of our boys.
lines.chosen: We haven't become straight/upstanding; that's the state of our boys.
lines.translated: We haven't become straight/upstanding; that's the state of our boys.
lines.note: The word 'sidha' can mean 'straight' or 'upright'; 'puthe' refers to sons/young men (literal). Exact nuance—moral uprightness, reputation, or behavior—is uncertain from context.

lines.original: Tu taan hune ee tang aagyi biba nindran udake
lines.literal: You have just now troubled (me) a lot, girl, by stealing sleep.
lines.natural: You've already been bothering me, girl, by stealing my sleep.
lines.slangAware: You've already been bothering me, girl, by stealing my sleep.
lines.chosen: You've already been bothering me, girl, by stealing my sleep.
lines.translated: You've already been bothering me, girl, by stealing my sleep.
lines.note: biba = girl / dear girl (see glossary).

lines.original: Sanu kai saal hogye naiyo changi tarah sutte
lines.literal: It's been many years we haven't slept well.
lines.natural: We haven't slept properly for many years.
lines.slangAware: We haven't slept properly for many years.
lines.chosen: We haven't slept properly for many years.
lines.translated: We haven't slept properly for many years.

lines.original: Naara thaar ke te vairi rakhan saarh ke rekaane
lines.literal: Raising slogans and keeping enemies, keeping (them) guarded.
lines.natural: They shout slogans and keep their enemies around, watching them.
lines.slangAware: They shout slogans and keep their enemies around, watching them.
lines.chosen: They shout slogans and keep their enemies close, watching them.
lines.translated: They shout slogans and keep their enemies close, watching them.
lines.ambiguity: Phrases like 'saarh ke rekaane' are regional and can mean 'keep under watch', 'keep on guard', or 'keep close/at hand.' I chose a conservative meaning — keeping enemies close/under watch.

lines.original: Peende motran te chahan pattu kaarh ke rekaane
lines.literal: Riding motors and keeping fans/servants pulled/tied and kept.
lines.natural: They ride motors and keep their followers/henchmen close.
lines.slangAware: They ride motors and keep their followers/henchmen close.
lines.chosen: They ride motors and keep their followers/henchmen close.
lines.translated: They ride motors and keep their followers/henchmen close.
lines.note: Several words here ('motran', 'chahan pattu') are uncertain in romanization; likely refers to vehicles ('motran' = motors/cars/bikes) and followers/servants. I avoided inventing specifics.
lines.ambiguity: Multiple plausible readings: could refer to riding vehicles, showing off cars/bikes, or keeping admirers/henchmen nearby. I used a conservative combined reading.

lines.original: Tere bull'an de maare ne main vi ek M laata
lines.literal: Because of your words' hits I too put an M.
lines.natural: Your words affected me; I also put an 'M'.
lines.slangAware: Your words affected me; I also put an 'M'.
lines.chosen: Your words affected me; I also put an 'M'.
lines.translated: Your words affected me; I also put an 'M'.
lines.note: The meaning of putting an 'M' is unclear in isolation — could be an initial/mark, a badge, or slang. Kept literal rather than guessing.
lines.ambiguity: Unclear what 'M laata' specifically refers to (initial, mark, gesture, or slang).

lines.original: Haale navi aa Ferrari jutti jhaad ke rekaane
lines.literal: Just now is a new Ferrari, (I) take off shoes and show.
lines.natural: I just got a new Ferrari; I kick off my shoes to show it off.
lines.slangAware: I just got a new Ferrari; I kick off my shoes to show it off.
lines.chosen: I just got a new Ferrari; I kick off my shoes to show it off.
lines.translated: I just got a new Ferrari; I kick off my shoes to show it off.
lines.note: 'jutti' = shoe; 'jhaad ke' here literally 'after taking off' and is used colloquially to suggest showing off.

lines.original: Dil todte main chhe chhe saak modte
lines.literal: When breaking hearts I twist/bend chhe chhe saak.
lines.natural: When I break hearts, I twist/bend 'chhe chhe saak'.
lines.slangAware: When I break hearts, I twist/bend 'chhe chhe saak'.
lines.chosen: When I break hearts, I twist/bend 'chhe chhe saak'.
lines.translated: When I break hearts, I twist/bend 'chhe chhe saak'.
lines.note: The phrase 'chhe chhe saak' is unclear — could be an onomatopoeic phrase, slang, or numeric ('chhe' = six). Kept literal to avoid inventing meaning.
lines.ambiguity: Multiple readings: literal sound/onomatopoeia, slang term, or number-based expression; exact sense unknown.

lines.original: Kataran wich atharan hor pakkian
lines.literal: In the lines there are eighteen more firm/packed.
lines.natural: There are eighteen more solid ones in the ranks.
lines.slangAware: There are eighteen more solid ones in the ranks.
lines.chosen: There are eighteen more solid ones in the ranks.
lines.translated: There are eighteen more solid ones in the ranks.

lines.original: Duron matha tekdiyan
lines.literal: From afar they bow/press their foreheads.
lines.natural: They bow their heads from afar.
lines.slangAware: They bow their heads from afar.
lines.chosen: They bow their heads from afar.
lines.translated: They bow their heads from afar.
lines.note: 'matha tekdiyan' literally means to touch/press the forehead (a gesture of bowing or respect); here implies showing deference from a distance.

lines.original: Mud mud dekh diyan
lines.literal: Turn-turn they look.
lines.natural: They keep turning around to look.
lines.slangAware: They keep turning around to look.
lines.chosen: They keep turning around to look.
lines.translated: They keep turning around to look.

lines.original: Assi naiyo ikk vaari takkiyan
lines.literal: We didn't (even) once look.
lines.natural: We didn't even glance once.
lines.slangAware: We didn't even glance once.
lines.chosen: We didn't even glance once.
lines.translated: We didn't even glance once.

lines.original: Fan ikko naar da main
lines.literal: Fan of only a woman I am.
lines.natural: I'm a fan of only one woman.
lines.slangAware: I'm a fan of only one woman.
lines.chosen: I'm a fan of only one woman.
lines.translated: I'm a fan of only one woman.
lines.note: Here 'naar' likely means 'woman/girl'. 'Fan' appears to be the English loanword 'fan' (admirer).

lines.original: Das vaari vaarda main
lines.literal: I say ten times
lines.natural: I say it ten times
lines.slangAware: I say it ten times
lines.chosen: I say it ten times
lines.translated: I say it ten times

lines.original: Ohde utton sau sau diyan gatthiyan
lines.literal: On/top of it there are hundreds-hundreds of bundles/knots
lines.natural: There are bundles of hundreds on it
lines.slangAware: There are bundles of hundreds on it
lines.chosen: There are bundles of hundreds on it
lines.translated: There are bundles of hundreds on it
lines.ambiguity: Could mean literal 'knots' or 'bundles'; may specifically refer to bundles of hundred-rupee notes (money) or to many knots/ties—context ambiguous.

lines.original: Time laggu mitne nu
lines.literal: It will take time to be erased/removed
lines.natural: It will take time to get rid of them
lines.slangAware: It will take time to get rid of them
lines.chosen: It will take time to get rid of them
lines.translated: It will take time to get rid of them
lines.ambiguity: 'mitne' is uncertain here — likely from 'mitaune' (to erase/remove) but could have other local senses; subject ('them') is inferred from prior line.

lines.original: Sanu thalle sittne nu
lines.literal: To make us sit down/brought down
lines.natural: To bring us down
lines.slangAware: To bring us down
lines.chosen: To bring us down
lines.translated: To bring us down
lines.ambiguity: 'sittne' interpreted as 'to put down / humiliate' — phrasing could vary regionally.

lines.original: Ikki vaari jor laa lai chhatti'an
lines.literal: Once, put force on the chests
lines.natural: Just once, put some force on the chest(s)
lines.slangAware: Just once, put some force on the chest(s)
lines.chosen: Just once, put some force on the chest(s)
lines.translated: Just once, put some force on the chest(s)
lines.ambiguity: 'chhatti'an' likely 'chhatiyaan' (chests); exact physical meaning (press, push, squeeze) of 'jor laa' is unclear—could be figurative or literal.

lines.original: Oh kudi jatt di fragrance door ton pehchane
lines.literal: That girl recognizes a jatt's fragrance from far away
lines.natural: That girl can recognize a jatt's scent from far away
lines.slangAware: That girl can recognize a jatt's scent from far away
lines.chosen: That girl can recognize a jatt's scent from far away
lines.translated: That girl can recognize a jatt's scent from far away
lines.note: jatt: jatt / tough Punjabi man (identity term); 'jatta'/'jatt' in the glossary refers to a masculine Punjabi identity.

lines.original: Asi aashqui ch nave ni shikari aa purane
lines.literal: We are not new hunters in love, we are old (ones)
lines.natural: We're not new to hunting in love; we're old hands
lines.slangAware: We're not new to hunting in love; we're old hands
lines.chosen: We're not new hunters in love; we're old ones
lines.translated: We're not new hunters in love; we're old ones
lines.note: Uses hunting metaphor ('shikari' = hunter) to mean experienced pursuers in romance ('aashqui' = love).

lines.original: Gal sukkda kehndi tu jadon supne ch aavein
lines.literal: You say my speech dries up when you come in dreams
lines.natural: You say I go speechless when you come in my dreams
lines.slangAware: You say I go speechless when you come in my dreams
lines.chosen: You say I go speechless when you come in my dreams
lines.translated: You say I go speechless when you come in my dreams
lines.ambiguity: 'Gal sukkda' is dialectal — interpreted as 'speech dries up / I become speechless'; structure assumes 'you say' (kehndi tu) that this happens when the addressee appears in dreams.

lines.original: Naddi ohdon di saundi aa pani rakh ke sirhane
lines.literal: That little river of those times sleeps with water kept by the pillow.
lines.natural: That little river used to sleep with water kept by the pillow.
lines.slangAware: That little river used to sleep with water kept by the pillow.
lines.chosen: That little river used to sleep with water kept by the pillow.
lines.translated: That little river used to sleep with water kept by the pillow.
lines.note: Literal reading: 'pani rakh ke sirhane' = 'keeping water by the pillow'. 'Naddi' most directly = 'small river'; may be metaphorical or refer to a person in context.
lines.ambiguity: Unclear whether 'Naddi' refers to an actual small river, a person, or is idiomatic.

lines.original: Main keha chalde rekaane sikke mitran de khote
lines.literal: I said, while walking, my friends' coins are counterfeit.
lines.natural: I said, as I walked, my friends' coins were fake.
lines.slangAware: I said, as I walked, my friends' coins were fake.
lines.chosen: I said, as I walked, my friends' coins were fake.
lines.translated: I said, as I walked, my friends' coins were fake.
lines.note: Here 'khote' can mean 'fake' (counterfeit) or literally 'donkeys' in different dialects; taken conservatively as 'fake'.
lines.ambiguity: Unclear what 'chalde rekaane' precisely means — likely 'while moving/going' or 'while things run'; 'rekaane' is not clear in isolation.

lines.original: Aa ke behnde aa rekaane cheene mitran de kothe
lines.literal: They come and sit, snatching rekaane from friends' kothe.
lines.natural: They come and sit and snatch 'rekaane' from friends' mansions.
lines.slangAware: They come and sit and snatch 'rekaane' from friends' mansions.
lines.chosen: They come and sit and snatch 'rekaane' from friends' mansions.
lines.translated: They come and sit and snatch 'rekaane' from friends' mansions.
lines.note: 'kothe' (kothas) commonly means large houses/mansions; 'cheene' = 'snatch/steal'. 'rekaane' is unclear and left untranslated in quotes.
lines.ambiguity: 'Rekaane' is ambiguous — could be a slang item, gesture, or object; context does not disambiguate.

lines.original: Tainu sohniye rekaane karaan hathan naal chhavaan
lines.literal: To you, beautiful, I make rekaane and touch with my hands.
lines.natural: Girl, I'll make 'rekaane' and touch you with my hands.
lines.slangAware: Girl, I'll make 'rekaane' and touch you with my hands.
lines.chosen: Girl, I'll make 'rekaane' and touch you with my hands.
lines.translated: Girl, I'll make 'rekaane' and touch you with my hands.
lines.note: 'sohniye' = affectionate address to a woman ('girl'/'beautiful one'). 'rekaane' remains unclear; rendered conservatively.
lines.ambiguity: 'Rekaane' could mean marks, gestures, moves, or a specific slang item — not clear from line alone.

lines.original: Jivein karde ne chhavaan sadde pind'an de barote
lines.literal: Like the chhavaan our villages' barote do.
lines.natural: Like the boys from our villages do the touching.
lines.slangAware: Like the boys from our villages do the touching.
lines.chosen: Like the boys from our villages do the touching.
lines.translated: Like the boys from our villages do the touching.
lines.note: 'pind'an' = 'villages'. 'chhavaan' = 'touches' or 'touching'. 'barote' is uncertain here — likely local lads/guys; left explanatory.
lines.ambiguity: 'Barote' could mean village lads, enforcers, or another local group; exact identity is unclear.

lines.original: Tera sau ton paar para hogyi dhilli kuj khaa ke
lines.literal: Your [something] beyond a hundred got loose after eating something.
lines.natural: Something of yours went slack past a hundred after eating something.
lines.slangAware: Something of yours went slack past a hundred after eating something.
lines.chosen: Something of yours went slack past a hundred after eating something.
lines.translated: Something of yours went slack past a hundred after eating something.
lines.note: 'dhilli para hogyi' = 'became loose/slack'. Phrase 'sau ton paar' = 'beyond a hundred' literally; full idiomatic sense is unclear.
lines.ambiguity: Unclear what bodily part or metaphor 'tera ...' refers to, and what 'sau ton paar' quantifies here.

lines.original: Teri 'taar da nazar gun powder chhuva ke
lines.literal: The sight of your 'taar' touched gunpowder.
lines.natural: The aim of your 'taar' brushed against the gunpowder.
lines.slangAware: The aim of your 'taar' brushed against the gunpowder.
lines.chosen: The aim of your 'taar' brushed against the gunpowder.
lines.translated: The aim of your 'taar' brushed against the gunpowder.
lines.note: 'nazar' = 'gaze' or 'sight'. 'taar' can mean 'string', 'wire', or figuratively 'line/trigger' — ambiguous; 'gun powder' is literal.
lines.ambiguity: 'Taar' could be literal (wire/string) or figurative (trigger/line); uncertain which is intended.

lines.original: Eh ton dar na rekaane dekh akhan wich paa ke
lines.literal: Don't be afraid of this, seeing rekaane put into the eyes.
lines.natural: Don't be afraid of this after seeing 'rekaane' put into the eyes.
lines.slangAware: Don't be afraid of this after seeing 'rekaane' put into the eyes.
lines.chosen: Don't be afraid of this after seeing 'rekaane' put into the eyes.
lines.translated: Don't be afraid of this after seeing 'rekaane' put into the eyes.
lines.note: 'akhan wich paa ke' = 'put into the eyes' (literal); could be metaphorical (e.g., a dazzling sight) or literal (apply to the eyes). 'rekaane' remains unclear.
lines.ambiguity: Could mean 'apply something (kohl/mark) to the eyes', 'make someone stare', or another local idiom involving 'rekaane'.

lines.original: Jadon chaldi Beretta dindi surma bana ke
lines.literal: When the Beretta moves, making surma (kohl).
lines.natural: When the Beretta moves, like kohl being drawn.
lines.slangAware: When the Beretta moves, like kohl being drawn.
lines.chosen: When the Beretta moves, like kohl being drawn.
lines.translated: When the Beretta moves, like kohl being drawn.
lines.note: Beretta is a gun make; surma = kohl (eye makeup). The verb 'dindi' in the original is unclear—interpreted here conservatively as an action of making/applying surma.
lines.ambiguity: Unclear agent and exact sense of 'dindi' (could be a name, slang, or verb form). It's not clear whether this line describes a person, the gun, or a metaphor.

lines.original: Kafi kar layi kamai kafi mitran ne udaalayi
lines.literal: Earned enough income, many friends have spent it.
lines.natural: I've earned enough; many friends have blown (spent) it.
lines.slangAware: I've earned enough; many friends have blown (spent) it.
lines.chosen: I've earned enough; many friends have spent it.
lines.translated: I've earned enough; many friends have spent it.

lines.original: Thode humble aa rekaane Lambo nivi karwali
lines.literal: Some are humble, (they) had the Lambo lowered.
lines.natural: Some (people) act humble; they had the Lambo lowered.
lines.slangAware: Some (people) act humble; they had the Lambo lowered.
lines.chosen: Some act humble; they had the Lambo lowered.
lines.translated: Some act humble; they had the Lambo lowered.
lines.note: Lambo = Lamborghini (car); 'nivi karwali' = had made/caused it to be lowered (suspension).
lines.ambiguity: Role of 'rekaane' is unclear (could mean 'people', a name, or dialectal particle).

lines.original: Nave shoran ton rekaane gaddi chaldi ni sidhi
lines.literal: Because of new shocks, the car doesn't run straight.
lines.natural: With the new shocks, the car doesn't drive straight.
lines.slangAware: With the new shocks, the car doesn't drive straight.
lines.chosen: With the new shocks, the car doesn't drive straight.
lines.translated: With the new shocks, the car doesn't drive straight.
lines.ambiguity: The word 'shoran' is likely 'shocks' (suspension) but could be another dialectal term.

lines.original: La daan meech ke ankhan nu back ganne di trolly
lines.literal: Holding the jaw tight, to the eyes like the back of a sugarcane trolley.
lines.natural: Jaw clenched, the eyes like the rear of a sugarcane trolley.
lines.slangAware: Jaw clenched, the eyes like the rear of a sugarcane trolley.
lines.chosen: Jaw clenched, the eyes like the rear of a sugarcane trolley.
lines.translated: Jaw clenched, the eyes like the rear of a sugarcane trolley.
lines.note: 'ganne di trolly' literally = sugarcane trolley/cart; likely a local/cultural image. Exact image intended is unclear.
lines.ambiguity: Phrase structure is unusual — unclear what 'La daan meech ke' exactly means (jaw/face posture or an idiom).

lines.original: Lagge gabru di tod kudi tutti payi nu aave
lines.literal: Seems like the gabru's break; the girl appears to have been broken/fallen apart.
lines.natural: It looks like the young man's blow; the girl seems to have been broken.
lines.slangAware: It looks like the young man's blow; the girl seems to have been broken.
lines.chosen: It looks like the young man's blow; the girl seems shattered.
lines.translated: It looks like the young man's blow; the girl seems shattered.
lines.note: gabru = young/tough man (see glossary). 'tod' can mean break/impact; 'tutti payi' = got broken/fallen apart.
lines.ambiguity: Could refer to physical breaking, emotional breakdown, or slangy boasting — exact sense is uncertain.

lines.original: Age naar di fasal jiven phutti payi nu aave
lines.literal: Like the crop of fire ahead, as if it has burst open.
lines.natural: Like a field of fire ahead, as if it's burst open.
lines.slangAware: Like a field of fire ahead, as if it's burst open.
lines.chosen: Like a field of fire ahead, as if it's burst open.
lines.translated: Like a field of fire ahead, as if it's burst open.
lines.note: 'naar' = fire; 'fasal' = crop/harvest — likely a metaphorical image.
lines.ambiguity: Metaphorical image is vague; could mean danger, spectacle, or something 'bursting' forward.

lines.original: Meri yaad di maari nu tainu hichki rekaane
lines.literal: Struck by my memory, you will get hiccups.
lines.natural: You'll get hiccups remembering me.
lines.slangAware: You'll get hiccups remembering me.
lines.chosen: You'll get hiccups remembering me.
lines.translated: You'll get hiccups remembering me.
lines.note: Uses the idea of being 'hit/struck' by memory of someone; 'hichki' = hiccup. Conservative reading chosen.

lines.original: Sachi barish de gaane wangu sutti payi nu aave
lines.literal: Like true rain's songs, it comes to the one lying asleep.
lines.natural: It comes to the person asleep like real songs of rain.
lines.slangAware: It comes to the person asleep like real songs of rain.
lines.chosen: It comes to the person asleep like real rain songs.
lines.translated: It comes to the person asleep like real rain songs.
lines.note: Sutti payi = lying asleep; 'barish de gaane' = songs of rain. Who 'it' refers to (memory, feeling, person) is not explicit.
lines.ambiguity: Unclear referent for 'it' (could be a feeling, memory, or person appearing in sleep).

lines.original: Bechain hogyi ikk kudi fan hogyi
lines.literal: One girl became restless, became a fan.
lines.natural: One girl grew restless and became a fan.
lines.slangAware: One girl grew restless and became a fan.
lines.chosen: One girl grew restless and became a fan.
lines.translated: One girl grew restless and became a fan.

lines.original: Aujle de kanna wich nattiyan
lines.literal: There are nattiyan in Aujle's ears.
lines.natural: Aujle has nattiyan in his ears.
lines.slangAware: Aujle has nattiyan in his ears.
lines.chosen: Aujle has nattiyan in his ears.
lines.translated: Aujle has nattiyan in his ears.
lines.note: Aujle likely refers to the artist Aujla (possessive). 'Nattiyan' is unclear—possibly earrings or another ornament; original word may be dialectal.
lines.ambiguity: Unclear meaning of 'nattiyan' (earrings, ornaments, or slang).

lines.original: Kari di ni care kude gaul de ni sher kude
lines.literal: Doesn't care about Kari, doesn't care about talk, (the) boys (are) lions.
lines.natural: She doesn't care about Kari or talk; the boys act like lions.
lines.slangAware: She doesn't care about Kari or talk; the boys act like lions.
lines.chosen: She doesn't care about it or about talk; the boys act like lions.
lines.translated: She doesn't care about it or about talk; the boys act like lions.
lines.note: Line appears colloquial and may contain misspellings/typos (e.g., 'Kari' could be 'kudi'); multiple readings possible.
lines.ambiguity: Several words/phrases are unclear or possibly mistyped, so the exact relationships and subjects are uncertain.

lines.original: Bhedaan hon baara baari kathiyan
lines.literal: There are twelve separations/splits.
lines.natural: There are twelve separations.
lines.slangAware: There are twelve separations.
lines.chosen: There are twelve separations.
lines.translated: There are twelve separations.
lines.note: Both 'bhedaan' (divisions/partings) and 'baara baari kathiyan' (twelve times / twelve folds) are somewhat idiomatic here.
lines.ambiguity: Unclear whether this refers to literal divisions, events happening twelve times, or a figurative count.

lines.original: Fan ikko naar da main
lines.literal: I am a fan of the same 'naar'.
lines.natural: I'm a fan of the same 'naar'.
lines.slangAware: I'm a fan of the same 'naar'.
lines.chosen: I’m a fan of just one girl
lines.translated: I’m a fan of just one girl
lines.note: 'naar' is ambiguous in this context — could mean a woman, a song/verse, or another dialectal term.
lines.ambiguity: Multiple plausible readings for 'naar' (woman, song, version), so exact meaning is uncertain.

lines.original: Das vaari vaarda main
lines.literal: I say it ten times.
lines.natural: I say it ten times.
lines.slangAware: I say it ten times.
lines.chosen: I say it ten times.
lines.translated: I say it ten times.

lines.original: Ohde utton sau sau diyan gatthiyan
lines.literal: Hundreds-hundreds of knots/bundles on top of him/her.
lines.natural: Hundreds upon hundreds of bundles over them.
lines.slangAware: Hundreds upon hundreds of bundles over them.
lines.chosen: Hundreds upon hundreds of bundles over them.
lines.translated: Hundreds upon hundreds of bundles over them.
lines.note: 'gatthiyan' literally means knots/bundles — can figuratively refer to bundles of money or stacked bundles.
lines.ambiguity: Could mean literal bundles/knots or figurative bundles (e.g., money); subject gender (him/her) from 'ohde' not specified.

lines.original: Time laggu mitne nu
lines.literal: It will take time to be erased.
lines.natural: It'll take time to be erased.
lines.slangAware: It'll take time to be erased.
lines.chosen: It'll take time to be erased.
lines.translated: It'll take time to be erased.
lines.ambiguity: The direct object of 'erase' (who or what will be erased) is not specified in the line.

lines.original: Sanu thalle sittne nu
lines.literal: To put us down.
lines.natural: To put us down.
lines.slangAware: To put us down.
lines.chosen: To put us down.
lines.translated: To put us down.
lines.ambiguity: Could mean 'to make us sit down' literally or 'to bring us down' (humiliate/defeat); context doesn't specify which.

lines.original: Ikki vaari jor laa lai chhatti'an
lines.literal: Once, put force, chhatti'an.
lines.natural: Just once, put some force, chhatti'an.
lines.slangAware: Just once, put some force, chhatti'an.
lines.chosen: Try putting in force just once, chhatti'an.
lines.translated: Try putting in force just once, chhatti'an.
lines.note: The word 'chhatti'an' is unclear here — it may be a slang term, a dialectal word, or a transcription variant.
lines.ambiguity: Unclear reading of 'chhatti'an' (could be a mis-transcription or regional slang); the exact object or action referred to by that word is uncertain.

lines.original: (Fan ikko naar da main)
lines.literal: (Fan ikko naar da main)
lines.natural: (I'm a fan of the same 'naar'.)
lines.slangAware: (I'm a fan of the same 'naar'.)
lines.chosen: (I'm a fan of the same 'naar'.)
lines.translated: (I'm a fan of the same 'naar'.)
lines.note: 'Fan' here appears to be the English word 'fan' used in Punjabi; 'naar' is ambiguous (could mean a shout/war-cry/slogan or something else).
lines.ambiguity: Unclear meaning of 'naar' in this context — could be 'shout/cry', 'slogan', or another regional term; overall line could mean 'I'm a fan of the same shout/slogan' or something else.

lines.original: (Das vaari vaarda main)
lines.literal: (I strike ten times)
lines.natural: (I strike ten times.)
lines.slangAware: (I strike ten times.)
lines.chosen: (I strike ten times.)
lines.translated: (I strike ten times.)

lines.original: (Ohde utton sau-sau diyan gatthiyan)
lines.literal: (Hundreds of bundles on him)
lines.natural: (Hundreds of bundles on him.)
lines.slangAware: (Hundreds of bundles on him.)
lines.chosen: (Hundreds of bundles on him.)
lines.translated: (Hundreds of bundles on him.)

lines.original: (Time laggu mitne nu)
lines.literal: (Time laggu mitne nu)
lines.natural: (It'll take time to be erased.)
lines.slangAware: (It'll take time to be erased.)
lines.chosen: (It'll take time to be erased.)
lines.translated: (It'll take time to be erased.)
lines.ambiguity: Same ambiguity as the non-parenthetical line: the object of 'erase' isn't specified.

lines.original: (Sanu thalle sittne nu)
lines.literal: (Sanu thalle sittne nu)
lines.natural: (To put us down.)
lines.slangAware: (To put us down.)
lines.chosen: (To put us down.)
lines.translated: (To put us down.)
lines.ambiguity: Could mean literal 'make us sit down' or figurative 'bring us down/humiliate'.

lines.original: (Ikki vaari jor laa lai chhatti'an)
lines.literal: One time, put force on the chhatti'an
lines.natural: Try it once — put some force on the chhatti'an.
lines.slangAware: Try it once — put some force on the chhatti'an.
lines.chosen: Try it once; put some force on chhatti'an.
lines.translated: Try it once; put some force on chhatti'an.
lines.note: Ikki vaari = 'once' or 'one time'. jor laa lai = 'apply force' / 'push hard'. 'chhatti'an' is unclear here — could be plural of chhati (chest) or a dialectal/slang word (or plural of chhat = roof). Kept untranslated in chosen due to this uncertainty.
lines.ambiguity: 'chhatti'an' has multiple plausible readings (e.g., 'chests', 'roofs', or slang); context doesn't conclusively disambiguate.
